theme.name: Ulysses
theme.description: Joyce's modernist masterpiece following Leopold Bloom through a single day (June 16, 1904) in Dublin. The novel reimagines Homer's Odyssey as an exploration of ordinary consciousness, transforming epic wandering into mundane perambulation. It examines how the mythic and quotidian interweave, how interior monologue reveals depths beneath banal surfaces, and how perspective rather than circumstance determines suffering or peace. The deck invites querents to see their own daily wanderings as odysseys, recognizing that wisdom lies not in transcending the ordinary but in one's relationship to it.
theme.creator: mykola bilokonsky, https://myk.pub

towers.name: Towers
towers.description: The heroic struggle, aspiration through opposition, Stephen's artistic ambition, the attempt to transcend through conflict. This suit embodies Mythic Resistance - the vertical thrust against the horizontal world, isolation in pursuit of higher truth, breaking rather than bending.

symbol.name: Tower on Hill
symbol.description: A stark, vertical tower with crenellations reaching upward, sitting atop a rounded hill. The tower stands alone, emphasizing both aspiration and fortification.
symbol.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 100" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="30" y="10" width="8" height="15" fill="currentColor"/><rect x="46" y="10" width="8" height="15" fill="currentColor"/><rect x="62" y="10" width="8" height="15" fill="currentColor"/><rect x="30" y="25" width="40" height="60" fill="currentColor"/><ellipse cx="50" cy="95" rx="35" ry="12" fill="currentColor"/></svg>

upright: Isolation in pursuit of higher truth

towers.visual_style: Angular and vertical. Sharp edges, dramatic contrasts, upward movement. Architecture of ambition - spires, ascents, precipices. Colors lean toward stark blacks and whites, cold blues, the monochrome of intellectual abstraction. Think Martello towers, medieval fortifications, Babel, Stephen's sharp profile against the sky.

keys.name: Keys
keys.description: Bloom's Odyssean patience, seeing the archetypal in the everyday, wisdom that flows rather than forces. This suit embodies Mythic Acceptance - unlocking understanding without breaking, recognizing patterns while flowing with them, access through gentle revelation.

symbol.name: Keys
symbol.description: Two keys crossed, their necks pointing downward and their teeth facing out.
symbol.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 100" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g transform="translate(50,50) rotate(-30) translate(-50,-50)"><circle cx="50" cy="15" r="12" fill="currentColor"/><rect x="48" y="27" width="4" height="68" fill="currentColor"/><rect x="52" y="78" width="7" height="4" fill="currentColor"/><rect x="52" y="84" width="9" height="4" fill="currentColor"/><rect x="52" y="90" width="5" height="4" fill="currentColor"/></g><g transform="translate(50,50) rotate(30) translate(-50,-50)"><circle cx="50" cy="15" r="12" fill="currentColor"/><rect x="48" y="27" width="4" height="68" fill="currentColor"/><rect x="41" y="78" width="7" height="4" fill="currentColor"/><rect x="39" y="84" width="9" height="4" fill="currentColor"/><rect x="43" y="90" width="5" height="4" fill="currentColor"/></g></svg>

keys.visual_style: Circular and flowing. Curved lines, cycles, spirals, mandalas. The geometric patterns of ancient wisdom - labyrinths, Celtic knots, circular journeys. Warm golds, deep blues, earth tones. Think Bloom's circular route through Dublin, the sun's circuit, ouroboros, the eternal return made peaceful.

nets.name: Nets
nets.description: Petty resentments, being trapped in the trivial, suffering the small indignities, Stephen's hangovers and guilt. This suit embodies Mundane Resistance - the nets flung at the soul through nationality, language, religion, and all the quotidian entanglements that feel crushing.

symbol.name: Net
symbol.description: A tangled net with regular mesh shown straight-on, emphasizing its constraining nature. The weave suggests both utility and entrapment.

upright: The nets flung at the soul
inverted: Nets woven by one's own hand

nets.visual_style: Tangled and claustrophobic. Irregular patterns, overlapping lines, compressed spaces, angles that trap the eye. The texture of discomfort - twisted sheets, crowded streets, grey skies. Muddy greens, dingy browns, the hung-over palette of morning-after Dublin. Think Stephen's unwashed body, dirty ashplants, tangled theological arguments.

kidneys.name: Kidneys
kidneys.description: The art of living well in the day-to-day, Bloom's kidney breakfast, his practical kindness, finding pleasure in the ordinary. This suit embodies Mundane Acceptance - embodied wisdom, the body's needs met with grace, nourishment and sensual pleasure in the everyday.

symbol.name: Kidney Bean
symbol.description: A kidney bean shape, organic and curved, suggesting both the literal organ and the warmth of embodied existence. Simple, rounded, comforting.

kidneys.visual_style: Organic and rounded. Soft curves, gentle textures, domestic spaces, bodily forms. The warmth of inhabited life - kitchen interiors, soft fabrics, flesh tones, food, beds. Warm browns, soft pinks, buttery yellows, the palette of comfort. Think Bloom's kidney breakfast, his bath, his sandwich, the cat's mew, Molly's warmth.

0.name: Homecoming
0.description: Homecoming - the return, the arrival, the recognition of where you began. Both Telemachus (Stephen's failed return to the tower) and Ithaca (Bloom's actual homecoming to Eccles Street). The ace contains the whole cycle within it - you arrive only to discover you never left, or you left only to discover you've arrived. The alpha-omega point where beginning and ending merge.

inverted: Return to what was left for good reason

0.visual_content: A threshold, a door, a key in a lock. Someone arriving or departing - the image should be ambiguous about which. The ace of each suit's symbol prominent, suggesting both seed and completion.

1.name: Replacement
1.description: Replacement - Joyce's radical move in Ulysses, abandoning Stephen for Bloom at episode 4. Not duality or partnership but substitution. The younger consciousness supplanted by the older, resistance replaced by acceptance, mind's primacy challenged by body. The two as succession rather than opposition - one protagonist gives way to another, one way of being exchanges for its opposite.

inverted: The new that fails to improve

1.visual_content: Two figures, one fading as the other solidifies. A young man dissolving into a middle-aged man. A tower giving way to a street. The moment of handoff, succession, substitution.

2.name: Trinity
2.description: Trinity - the three-in-one that stabilizes what Replacement threatened. Stephen (mind/son), Bloom (body/father), Molly (flesh/spirit). Joyce's heretical trinity that completes by triangulation. Morning/Day/Night. Resistance/Acceptance/Affirmation. The anxiety of the binary resolved through the third term. Three creates the system of relationships that makes the structure stable.

upright: Father, Son, and Holy Ghost recast
inverted: System that traps rather than contains

2.visual_content: Three figures arranged in relationship - not a line but a triangle. Morning light, afternoon sun, night darkness. Three spaces: tower, street, bed. The three-in-one made visible.

3.name: The City
3.description: The City - Dublin itself as foundation, bounded space, the stable four-square container for wandering. The Liffey, the bridges, streets as grid, the civic structure. After Trinity comes the space they move through. Four corners, four directions, the city as stage and character. The foundation that grounds the mythic in the mundane, the map that makes wandering legible.
3.visual_content: Dublin from above - the Liffey bisecting, bridges connecting, streets as grid. The four-square city, bounded and mapped. Corners, directions, the stable foundation for all that follows.

4.name: The Outsider
4.description: The Outsider - the one who doesn't fit the four-square city. Stephen the autistic intellectual who has no language for why he's always outside. Bloom the Jew-on-father's-side never fully accepted. The disruption in traditional tarot becomes the person who disrupts by existing. The five-pointed star that won't tessellate with the four-square world. Not conflict as action but as being.

inverted: Exile as chosen rather than imposed

4.visual_content: A figure at the edge, outside the frame, not quite fitting into the scene. The five-pointed star among four-square geometry. Someone marked as different, watching from outside.

5.name: Identity
5.description: Identity - forging in the smithy of the soul what doesn't yet exist. Finding the others - Bloom and Stephen's karass, the recognition that creates the category. Not just discovering you're not alone but discovering what you ARE through recognizing them. Stephen at Portrait's end declaring he'll create his people's uncreated conscience. Identity as made not found, as collective discovery not individual certainty.
5.visual_content: Two people recognizing each other across distance. A forge, a smithy, something being made. The moment of 'oh, there you are' - identity discovered and created simultaneously.

6.name: Asymmetry
6.description: Asymmetry - what cannot be evenly split. Trinity (3) and City (4) in permanent tension, making seven. The realization of being outnumbered. Bloom alone against the Citizen's pub. Stephen in any room. Seven as the unresolvable, the permanent minority, the odd one out. Not the crossroads of traditional tarot but the recognition that the choice was always unequal, the sides never balanced.

upright: Three and four that won't balance

6.visual_content: An uneven split, an imbalanced scale. One against many. Three plus four making seven - the parts visible, the asymmetry clear. Something that cannot be divided evenly.

7.name: History
7.description: History - the nightmare Stephen tries to awake from and the ocean that will one day bring him home as he grows into Joyce. The accumulated weight of everything that happened, which is both burden and buoyancy. The inescapable record, the pattern that repeats. 'History is a nightmare' / 'The heaventree of stars' - both true simultaneously. Not movement or mastery but the recognition of all that came before.
7.visual_content: Layers of time visible simultaneously. The past pressing into present. A young man struggling against waves that will eventually carry him home. History as both weight and current.

8.name: Invitation
8.description: Invitation - the threshold, the moment just before recognition, the offer extended. Bloom standing in the garden with Stephen under stars. Molly nearly awake, about to speak. The breath before 'yes.' Not fulfillment but the space just before it, the invitation to see what's always been there. The door opened but not yet walked through.
8.visual_content: An open doorway, a threshold. Someone about to speak or step through. The moment of almost, the space of possibility. Stars overhead, an offer in the air.

9.name: Recurrence
9.description: Recurrence - not completion that overflows but completion that reveals itself as beginning again. The cycle made explicit. Finishing Ulysses only to want to start again. Molly's 'yes' echoing back to Howth Head. 'Commodius vicus of recirculation.' The ten as the discovery that the ace was always already there, that arrival was departure, that every ending is invitation to begin.
9.visual_content: A spiral, a cycle visible, ouroboros. The end becoming the beginning. Ten symbols arranged in a circle, the last touching the first. Riverrun, the eternal return.

10.name: Child
10.description: Child - Stephen, the young, the unformed, the resistant. The artist before creation, the son seeking the father, the intellectual in agony. The one who carries potential but hasn't yet learned to manifest it. All youth, uncertainty, refusal, brilliance turned inward. The child who must grow but doesn't know how.
10.visual_content: A young man, brilliant but struggling. Books, tower, the sea. Someone at the beginning who doesn't yet know the middle or end. The child in adult form.

11.name: Father
11.description: Father - Bloom, the paternal, the one who tends and accepts. The wanderer who has learned patience. The outsider who has made peace with outsiderness. The one who offers shelter, makes cocoa, picks up the fallen. Mature masculine not as dominance but as care, wisdom not as knowing but as witnessing.
11.visual_content: A middle-aged man, kind-eyed, weary but patient. Making cocoa, picking someone up, walking through the city. The father who offers what can't be forced.

12.name: Mother
12.description: Mother - Molly, the maternal, the body, the keeper of home and truth. The one who sees through everyone's nonsense. Embodied wisdom, sensual honesty, the earth itself. The feminine principle not as passive but as foundational, not as nurturing but as real. The mother who holds the bed and the final word.
12.visual_content: A woman in bed, conscious and powerful. Not waiting but being. The body's wisdom, flesh made eloquent. The mother who holds everything together by being.

13.name: Affirmation
13.description: Affirmation - Yes. The final word, Molly's ultimate acceptance, the culmination of all cycles. Not just agreement but affirmation of existence itself despite everything. The yes that echoes from Howth Head forward and backward through all time. The highest court card because it transcends the personal - it's the universe saying yes to itself.
13.visual_content: The word YES visible, large, flowering. Molly on Howth Head, flowers in her hair. The affirmation as both memory and present. The yes that contains everything.

transversal.name: Vico's Cycle
transversal.description: Giambattista Vico's ricorso - history as the eternal recurrence of four ages, the deep structure Joyce wound through Finnegans Wake and let hum beneath Ulysses. Laid across the deck it asks of every card not what hour it is but what age of the world its spirit belongs to: the thunderclap of the Gods, the heroes' monumental dusk, the prose of common men, or the dissolving return that begins it all again. It turns at the pace of epochs, deliberately out of phase with the single Dublin day the cards otherwise inhabit.
transversal.ordering_rationale: Vico's sequence is fixed and cyclical: the Age of Gods (theocratic, born of terror and thunder) gives way to the Age of Heroes (aristocratic, monumental), then the Age of Men (democratic, prosaic, the language of the marketplace), then the ricorso - a chaotic dissolution that returns the world to the gods and begins the corso again. The order descends from the sacred and primal toward the human and worn, then breaks and recirculates. Four ages laid over a single day put the deck permanently out of phase with its own clock, which is the point.

gods.name: Age of Gods
gods.description: The theocratic dawn: the sacred sensed as terror and thunder before it can be understood.

inverted: the irrational mistaken for the divine

gods.visual_motif: Storm-dark sky split by lightning; vast primal scale, a small figure under an enormous sacred weather, thunder-grey and gold-white; the air charged before meaning, the numinous as both threat and promise.

heroes.name: Age of Heroes
heroes.description: The aristocratic dusk: monumental ambition, the great deed and the great name, and the cruelty beneath them.
heroes.visual_motif: Monumental forms in burnished bronze and twilight purple; long heroic shadows, statuary stillness, a single figure rendered larger than life against a declining heroic sun; grandeur edged with the chill of pride.

men.name: Age of Men
men.description: The democratic daylight: the prose of the marketplace, common speech, the civic and ordinary.

inverted: the marketplace as the measure of all things

men.visual_motif: Flat even daylight over a crowded ordinary street; muted civic browns and newsprint greys, human scale, many figures and none exalted; the unheroic clarity of a working afternoon.

ricorso.name: The Ricorso
ricorso.description: The dissolution and return: collapse into night and dream, the river running back to its source.
ricorso.visual_motif: Forms melting into river-fog and dream; deep night-blues bleeding into a faint dawn, dissolving edges, water and reflection; the world coming apart softly to begin again - riverrun, the heaventree, the close that loops to the open.

major_arcana.story: The Major Arcana follows Joyce's literary journey from A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man through the eighteen episodes of Ulysses on June 16, 1904, concluding with the novel's geographical signature and the promise of Finnegans Wake. It traces Stephen Dedalus's morning of intellectual crisis, Leopold Bloom's odyssey through Dublin as he navigates grief and cuckolding with remarkable equanimity, their convergence in Nighttown, Bloom's return home, and Molly's affirming soliloquy. The journey asks: how do we survive ordinariness? How do consciousness and compassion interact? What is the relationship between mythic pattern and mundane reality?
major_arcana.visual_style: The Major Arcana should feel literary and layered, incorporating both the realistic and the experimental. Early cards (Portrait, Stephen's morning) lean modernist and austere - sharp lines, intellectual abstraction, the alienation of consciousness. Bloom's cards are warmer, more embodied, with attention to physical detail and domestic spaces. The middle episodes grow increasingly stylistically experimental - journalistic fragments for Aeolus, musical notation for Sirens, gigantism for Cyclops. Circe explodes into hallucinatory expressionism. The final cards (Eumaeus, Ithaca, Penelope) move through exhaustion toward catechism toward stream-of-consciousness release. Colors throughout should evoke both Joyce's Dublin (grey stone, brown Liffey, green Ireland, the gold of Guinness) and the Mediterranean epic (wine-dark seas, bronze and gold). Each card should feel like it could be a book cover for that episode.

meaning.upright: Stephen Dedalus before Ulysses, declaring non serviam but untested by the actual day - the artist who vows to forge in the smithy of his soul the uncreated conscience of his race. The name holds both senses at once: the maker at the anvil and the counterfeiter of a self not yet made. Awakening before testing, rejection as the necessary first step, pure potential with nothing yet struck. The forge is lit; the journey is about to start.
meaning.inverted: Ambition as arrogance untested by reality; rejection as mere negation. The forger who only counterfeits - the artist who cannot create, only theorize, a forge that produces nothing but declaration. Certainty as delusion, independence curdled to isolation from anything that might teach.

visuals.detailed_description: Stephen Dedalus stands at the edge of a pier, back three-quarters to the viewer, looking out toward a stylized sea that suggests both literal ocean and metaphorical possibility. Behind him, rendered in sharp architectural lines: the spire of a church, the dome of a university, the grey stone of Dublin. These are declining in detail as they recede, becoming more abstract. Before him, the sea is suggested through modernist waves - geometric, almost cubist. In the sky above, faint wing-shapes in cloud or light, not quite formed. Stephen himself is thin, angular, dressed in black, his profile sharp against the grey-green Irish sky. The overall composition suggests threshold, rejection, and untested certainty. A book under one arm. The nets of nationality, language, religion are suggested as actual nets in the architecture behind him, but he doesn't see them yet.

meaning.upright: 8am at the Martello Tower. Buck Mulligan mocks the mass while shaving, Stephen haunted by his dead mother and his refusal to pray at her deathbed. Living with Mulligan and Haines under sufferance. Stephen surrenders his tower key - the cracked lookingglass of a servant. Dispossession, guilt, morning after excess, intellectual cutting and being cut.  Uneasy cohabitation with those who don't truly see you. Guilt over the dead that cannot be resolved through false consolation. Refusing what would comfort but compromise. The cracked mirror showing truth through its breaking. Dispossession as necessary loss. Morning clarity after night of confusion.
meaning.inverted: Guilt becoming paralysis rather than insight. Refusal as mere negation without alternative. Art that only reflects brokenness without transformation. Dispossession as identity rather than circumstance. Excess without the learning it might bring. Cutting others to feel something. Martyrdom to pride.

visuals.detailed_description: The Martello Tower dominates - round, stone, solid, taking up perhaps half the card. At its top, Buck Mulligan in a yellow dressing gown holds a shaving bowl high, his posture mock-priestly, face theatrical, performing. Below on the spiral stairs, Stephen in black descends, thin, haunted, his face showing the 'agenbite of inwit' - perhaps his mother's face reflected in a cracked mirror he carries or in a window. A large old-fashioned key is prominent in the foreground, either in Buck's hand or on the stone. Behind everything, the grey-green Irish Sea, morning light cold and clear. The tower is both fortress and prison. A milk woman might be suggested at the base - Ireland itself, not recognizing her own.

meaning.upright: 10am at Mr. Deasy's school. Stephen teaches history to indifferent boys, receives his wages, endures Deasy's antisemitic commentary. History is a nightmare from which I am trying to awake. Truth is a shout in the street. Teaching without belief, working within the system while rejecting it, the exhaustion of young adulthood.  Teaching what you don't believe to earn what you need. The gap between generations made visible. History as burden pressing down. Confronting prejudice with interior resistance. Truth found not in lecture halls but in the street's spontaneity. Wages earned without dignity but earned nonetheless. The exhaustion that comes from doing necessary work while knowing its limitations.
meaning.inverted: Teaching as mere performance, the words empty. Wages as chains keeping you in place. History as excuse rather than nightmare to escape. Complicity with prejudice through silence. Generational conflict as permanent identity. Nihilism disguised as sophistication. The system as inevitable, resistance as pose. Exhaustion as permanent condition not temporary state.

visuals.detailed_description: Interior of a Victorian classroom, all dark wood and oppressive browns. Stephen stands at a blackboard with a history lesson written on it (perhaps something about Pyrrhus, his famous victory), but his face shows disconnection - he's not present to his own teaching. Before him, several boys slouch at desks, bored, restless, looking away. In the foreground, large and dominating, Mr. Deasy sits at a desk counting coins - wages - his face showing satisfaction and prejudice. On the walls: maps of empire, history charts, the accumulated weight of the past. Through a window, bright light and the suggestion of the street outside where real life happens. Perhaps a cat in the window - the 'shout in the street.' The composition emphasizes Stephen trapped between the dead past (Deasy, history) and indifferent future (boys) while truth exists outside.

meaning.upright: 11am on Sandymount Strand. Stephen walks alone testing Berkeley - shut your eyes and see. Memories of Paris, dying mother, failures. Reading the signatures of all things. Philosophy tested by embodied experience - he picks his nose, urinates, writes poetry on torn envelope. Solitary contemplation, the beach as liminal space, reality as protean and mutable.  Solitary contemplation as necessary practice. Reality as mutable, changeable, not fixed. Reading the signatures - seeing the meaning in things. Body and mind intertwined, not separate. The beach as space between, neither land nor sea. Philosophy must be tested by lived experience. Small acts of creation from whatever materials are at hand. Walking as form of thinking.
meaning.inverted: Contemplation as avoidance of action. Reality dissolved into relativism where nothing is real. Over-reading until meaning disappears into interpretation. Mind severed from body, each denying the other. Liminality as permanent condition, never arriving. Philosophy as excuse for inaction. Creation that remains only fragmentary. Walking in circles without destination.

visuals.detailed_description: A beach stretches across the card, but nothing is quite stable. The horizon line between sand, sea, and sky wavers or blurs. Stephen walks along the strand, thin figure in black, at one moment his eyes are closed (shut your eyes and see), at another open and looking down. Around him, the beach is dense with signatures - shells, seaweed, a dead dog, tide pools that reflect not just sky but also text or philosophical concepts. In the foreground, a hand (disconnected, Stephen's) writes on a torn envelope with a pencil stub. Behind everything, the sea changes: is it grey? Green? Blue? It shifts. The philosophical concept of 'ineluctable modality of the visible' might be suggested through the visual treatment itself - solid objects that also seem somehow transparent or mutable. A drowned man might be suggested in the waves. Everything is liminal, between states, protean.

meaning.upright: 8am at 7 Eccles Street. Leopold Bloom prepares breakfast, loves the inner organs of beasts and fowls. Buys a kidney, feeds the cat, brings breakfast to Molly in bed. A letter from Blazes Boylan - today is the day - but Bloom proceeds anyway. Reads, defecates, plans his day. Domestic routine as anchor, the body's needs attended to, acceptance of circumstance.  Domestic routine as anchor in uncertainty. The body's needs attended to with care and pleasure. Practical kindness as daily practice. Sensual pleasure in the ordinary - food, warmth, the cat's purr. Knowing what is coming but proceeding anyway with equanimity. Morning rituals that ground the day. Home as imperfect haven. Acceptance not as resignation but as realistic engagement with life.
meaning.inverted: Routine as anesthetic against feeling. Body's needs as distraction from what must be faced. Kindness as avoidance of necessary confrontation. Pleasure seeking to escape reality. Proceeding despite knowledge as passivity not courage. Ritual as empty repetition without meaning. Home as prison not haven. Acceptance as resignation, giving up rather than taking in.

visuals.detailed_description: Bloom stands at a stove in a small kitchen, the morning sun streaming through lace curtains. A kidney sizzles in a black pan, rendered in loving detail - you can almost smell it. At his feet, a black cat winds between his legs, its back arched in pleasure, very present and physical. The kitchen is modest but warm: a teapot, bread on a board, butter in a dish, the Agendath Netaim leaflet visible. Through a doorway, we see Molly in bed, her dark hair spread on the pillow, not threatened but substantial, real. The breakfast tray waits to be brought. On her bedside table, a letter with 'Boylan' visible on the envelope. But the mood is not one of dread but of tender acceptance - Bloom knows and proceeds. The color palette is warm browns, yellows, the golden light of morning. Everything is tangible, embodied, real - the opposite of Stephen's abstractions.

meaning.upright: 10am, Bloom wanders collecting a love letter from Martha Clifford, buying soap, observing church service, ordering Molly's lotion, anticipating his bath. Various lotus-eating narcotics: religion, correspondence, chemistry, bathing. Gentle escapism, small pleasures, drifting through the day, multiple identities held lightly.  Gentle escapism as necessary respite. Small pleasures as sustenance. Drifting through the day with purpose underneath. Multiple identities held lightly without losing the self. Anticipation of comfort as its own pleasure. Observing without harsh judgment. The chemistry of consolation. The bath that awaits.
meaning.inverted: Escapism as addiction to forgetting. Pleasure as avoidance of necessary pain. Drifting as aimlessness without anchor. Fragmented identity that cannot cohere. Anticipation never fulfilled, always deferred. Observation as disconnection from life. Consolation as replacement for engagement. Drowning in the bath meant for cleansing.

visuals.detailed_description: The card has a soft, layered quality - perhaps multiple transparent scenes overlapping. Bloom in various poses: reaching for a letter at the post office (addressed to Henry Flower), standing outside a church watching communicants through a doorway, examining a bar of lemon soap in a chemist's, walking past Sweny's pharmacy. In the background or as a large element, an empty bathtub with steam rising, suggesting promised comfort. The letter from Martha visible with purple prose partially readable. Chemistry bottles with flower names as labels. A church interior glimpsed but Bloom always outside it. The overall palette is warm but slightly hazy - the narcotic quality of gentle escape. Everything suggests anticipation, multiplicity, drift without desperation.

meaning.upright: 11am at Glasnevin Cemetery. Bloom attends Paddy Dignam's funeral. Death saturates - Bloom's father's suicide, his son Rudy who died at 11 days, Parnell's grave. Contemplating mortality, decomposition, memory. Excluded from the men's conversation, his Jewishness marking him as other. Confronting death, grief carried quietly, the outsider's perspective.  Confronting mortality with clear eyes. Grief carried quietly, with dignity. Exclusion from community as position from which to see truth. The body's inevitable decay acknowledged without horror. Memory as form of resurrection. Sitting with death rather than fleeing. The outsider's perspective as gift. Loss compounding loss met with continued living.
meaning.inverted: Mortality as obsession that prevents living. Grief as permanent identity. Exclusion as chosen isolation from any community. Morbid fascination replacing healthy acknowledgment. Memory as haunting rather than honoring. Death wish disguised as contemplation. Outsider position as superiority. Loss as excuse for not continuing.

visuals.detailed_description: The scene is Glasnevin Cemetery under grey Dublin skies. Celtic crosses and stone angels populate the background, rain-darkened and ancient. In the middle ground, a group of men in black stand around an open grave - Paddy Dignam's funeral. Among them but slightly to the side, Bloom stands, observant, excluded from their conversation. His face shows contemplation rather than grief - he's thinking about death itself. Ghosted or suggested in the composition: his father's grave, a tiny coffin (Rudy), Parnell's monument. A funeral carriage with horses. The overall mood is somber but not despairing - Bloom meets death with scientific curiosity and quiet compassion. The stones themselves seem to have presence. Rain beginning to fall or having just fallen, everything wet and grey-green.

meaning.upright: Noon at the newspaper office. Bloom tries to secure an advertisement, Stephen arrives with Deasy's letter. Journalists pontificate about oratory and rhetoric. Editor Myles Crawford rejects Bloom's pitch. Stephen tells his parable of the plums. Hot air, rhetoric, winds of speech going nowhere. Multiple voices, no consensus, noise masking emptiness.  Rhetoric and hot air recognized as such. The press as organ of opinion, not truth. Multiple voices creating cacophony, preventing consensus. Persuasion attempted with honesty, rejected nonetheless. Words as wind - powerful but insubstantial. Professional disappointment met with resilience. The crowd's judgment as fickle. Noise that, once recognized, can be navigated.
meaning.inverted: Rhetoric as manipulation without care for truth. The press as propaganda machine. Cacophony as deliberate confusion, preventing clarity. Persuasion as coercion, force disguised as reason. Words as weapons against the vulnerable. Disappointment becoming bitterness and cynicism. Mob rule accepted as democracy. Emptiness embraced, celebrated even.

visuals.detailed_description: The card is fragmented by newspaper headlines that cut across the image like the episode's structure: SOPHIST WALLOPS HAUGHTY HELEN, SAD, etc. A newspaper office: printing presses in the background, papers everywhere, the machinery of opinion visible. In the foreground, multiple men arguing - journalists with theatrical gestures, Myles Crawford dominating, his finger pointing in dismissal. Bloom stands with his ad pitch, professionally dressed, being rejected. Stephen sits or stands to the side, young and observant, about to tell his parable. Papers blow across the scene as if in a wind. Speech bubbles or text fragments suggest the cacophony. The overall effect is of noise, movement, rhetoric, hot air - lots of talking, nothing said. Moses on Pisgah might be suggested in background imagery.

meaning.upright: 1pm, Bloom wanders hungry toward lunch. Observes people eating, Dublin's denizens as cannibals. Helps a blind boy cross the street. Thinks about Molly, Boylan, food, sex. Avoids Burton's restaurant carnivores, opts for gorgonzola sandwich and burgundy at Davy Byrne's. Hunger both physical and emotional, choosing gentler options, the moderate path.  Hunger both physical and emotional acknowledged. Observing the feeding frenzy without joining its violence. Small acts of kindness in passing. Choosing gentler options when harsher ones available. Body's needs driving consciousness but not tyrannizing it. Memory and appetite intertwined naturally. The moderate path as wisdom not cowardice. Nourishment found that satisfies without excess.
meaning.inverted: Hunger as all-consuming obsession. Seeing everyone as predator or prey. Kindness as performance for self-regard. Moderation as deprivation, denying legitimate needs. Body's needs tyrannizing mind and spirit. Memory as torment preventing present satisfaction. The middle path as cowardice, refusing to choose. Never satisfied, always hungry.

visuals.detailed_description: Dublin's lunch hour crowds fill the card. In the foreground, Bloom walks, his face thoughtful, hungry. Around him, people eat - mouths open, food visible, the act of consumption emphasized. One vignette shows him helping a blind boy cross the street, a moment of gentleness. To one side, Burton's restaurant shows men eating like cannibals - messy, violent, carnivorous. In contrast, Davy Byrne's pub glows warmly - civilized, moderate. A gorgonzola sandwich and a glass of burgundy prominent, rendered lovingly. Bloom's thoughts suggested through ghosted imagery: Molly's body, Boylan's swagger, food and sex intermingled. The overall palette is warm - reds, browns, the colors of appetite. The peristaltic process suggested visually somehow - consumption, digestion, incorporation.

meaning.upright: 2pm at the National Library. Stephen presents his Hamlet theory to literary establishment - Shakespeare as Prince Hamlet avenging his dead son through art. Autobiography as key to creation. Stephen navigates between Aristotelian realism and Platonic idealism. Theory rejected but Stephen passes through. Dense literary criticism, dialectical argument, intellectual combat.  Navigating between extremes, between the rock and the whirlpool. Theory and performance as necessary steps toward creation. Art emerging from autobiography, from lived grief. Dead sons and living fathers in dialogue. Intellectual combat as sharpening, not destruction. The artist explaining himself as part of becoming himself. Passing through despite rejection, the navigation itself as victory.
meaning.inverted: Theory as fortress preventing actual creation. Performance as substitute for genuine work. Autobiography as prison, unable to transcend what happened. The dead instrumentalized for argument. Combat as only mode, destroying connection. Explanation as compensation for what cannot be made. Passing through without learning, unchanged. Paralyzed between options, unable to choose.

visuals.detailed_description: The National Library's reading room with its shelves and domed ceiling forms the setting. Stephen stands in the center, young, arguing passionately with several older literary men who sit in judgment - AE Russell's mystical face, John Eglinton's skepticism, others. Books are everywhere, stacked, shelved, the accumulated weight of literature. Ghosted or suggested: Shakespeare's face, Hamlet in black, the ghost of Hamlet's father. The theoretical argument is suggested through text fragments or visual representations of the two dangers: on one side, Scylla as a sharp rock formation (Aristotelian realism), on the other side, Charybdis as a whirlpool (Platonic idealism). Stephen navigates between them. Buck Mulligan crashes in from the side, theatrical. The overall mood is of intellectual combat, dense theory, the library as battleground. Despite rejection, Stephen is passing through.

meaning.upright: 3pm, Dublin as labyrinth. Nineteen vignettes following various characters as paths cross unknowingly. Father Conmee's trip, the viceregal cavalcade, Bloom buying a book for Molly, Stephen browsing, Boylan buying fruit. The city itself as protagonist. Simultaneity, paths crossing, patterns emerging from chaos, community without connection.  The city as system, larger than any individual. Paths crossing unknowingly, connections unseen but real. Everyone wandering their own journey simultaneously. The whole exceeding its parts. Small moments in aggregate creating meaning. Patterns emerging from apparent chaos. Community without requiring conscious connection. The labyrinth as map of life itself.
meaning.inverted: The city as maze without exit or center. Near-misses as tragedy, connection always missed. Wandering as lost, without purpose or destination. Simultaneity as fragmentation preventing coherence. Moments that don't cohere, remaining disparate. The whole as mere collection without meaning. Chaos unresolved into pattern. Isolation masquerading as proximity.

visuals.detailed_description: The card shows Dublin as if from above, a map come to life. Streets radiate out like a labyrinth, the Liffey bisecting. Multiple vignettes occur simultaneously: Father Conmee walking and blessing, the viceregal cavalcade processing in pomp, Bloom purchasing Sweets of Sin in a bookshop for Molly, Stephen browsing book carts, Boylan buying fruit for his assignation, and many others moving through their afternoon. Paths cross without the characters knowing - Bloom and Stephen pass near each other but don't meet yet. Clocks visible in different locations all show 3pm. The overall effect is of a living city, an organism, patterns emerging from apparent chaos. The labyrinth is visible in the street layout itself. Everything is in motion, crossing, the wandering rocks of the title suggested through the moving figures who might collide but don't.

meaning.upright: 4pm at the Ormond Hotel bar. Bloom has lunch while Boylan passes by on his way to Molly. Barmaids Lydia Douce and Mina Kennedy sing and flirt. Music everywhere - songs, tapped glasses, tuning fork. Simon Dedalus sings. Bloom writes to Martha. The moment of betrayal approached obliquely through music. Seduction by sound, distraction through beauty, knowing and not quite knowing.  Music as consolation when other comfort fails. Seduction by sound and beauty, gentle and not violent. The moment of betrayal approached obliquely, bearable through indirection. Multiple melodies simultaneous, fugal. Language as music, meaning in sound not just semantics. Distraction through beauty as mercy. Resonance and vibration as forms of truth. Knowing and not quite knowing as sustainable position.
meaning.inverted: Music as narcotic preventing necessary feeling. Seduction as manipulation through beauty. Betrayal dwelt upon, the wound picked at. Cacophony instead of harmony, noise not music. Language losing meaning entirely in sound. Beauty as cruelty, offering what it cannot deliver. Resonance as dissonance, vibration as pain. Willful ignorance disguised as acceptance.

visuals.detailed_description: The Ormond Hotel bar glows in bronze and gold. Two barmaids - one bronze-haired (Miss Douce), one gold (Miss Kennedy) - are prominent, singing, their mouths open in song, flirtatious and aware of their power. Music is made visible throughout: actual musical notation floating in the air, sound waves radiating from a tuning fork, the vibration of tapped glass. Bloom sits at a table, writing a letter to Martha, his face showing awareness of what's happening (Boylan and Molly) but choosing the oblique approach. Simon Dedalus stands singing, his voice suggested through visual effects. Outside the window, we see or sense hoofbeats - Boylan passing on his way to 7 Eccles Street. Musical instruments are present - a piano, perhaps. The whole card should feel like synesthesia - sound made visible, the seduction of music palpable. The overture: 'Bronze by gold heard the hoofirons, steelyringing' somehow suggested.

meaning.upright: 5pm at Barney Kiernan's pub. Bloom encounters Irish nationalists including the violent Citizen. They drink, argue about nationalism, violence, identity. Bloom's reasonable liberalism enrages the Citizen. A biscuit tin thrown at Bloom as he flees. Gigantism, mock-heroic, the Citizen's single-eyed nationalism as blindness.  Confronting violence with reason, courage despite fear. Reasoned response to unreason as moral necessity. The outsider speaking truth to power. Nationalism as blindness, the single eye that cannot see depth. Alcohol fueling rage, revealing what's underneath. Narrow escape as victory of sorts. Standing up when silence would be easier. The cyclops revealed as small despite his size.
meaning.inverted: Violence as inevitable, reason as provocation deserving attack. Reason itself as cowardice, talking instead of acting. Otherness as permanent exclusion justifying violence. Nationalism as only valid identity, the single eye as purity. Rage as righteous, justified by grievance. Escape as cowardice not wisdom. Speech as futility, words as nothing. Blindness as chosen, preferred to complex vision.

visuals.detailed_description: The card is split between gigantic parody and squalid reality. The Citizen dominates - enormous, one huge eye, dressed in mock-heroic Irish nationalist garb (green, Celtic symbols), his face purple with rage and drink. He throws a biscuit tin. Around him, men in a pub drinking, listening, the crowd that enables violence. Bloom stands or backs away, small but dignified, reasonable, his face showing both fear and moral certainty. His Jewishness somehow suggested. In the background or overlaid: mock-heroic imagery - Cuchulainn, Finn MacCool, ancient Irish giants and heroes, but rendered in parody. The gap between the epic pretension and the actual violence of throwing a tin at a reasonable man should be stark. A dog (Garryowen) barks. Pint glasses, sawdust, the pub's squalor. The single eye of the Cyclops prominent - nationalism as blindness.

meaning.upright: 8pm on Sandymount Strand. Gerty MacDowell sits on rocks with friends, watches church service, thinks in purple prose of romance novels. She displays herself to Bloom, who masturbates watching her. She limps away. Both lonely, seeking connection through distance. Fireworks from the bazaar punctuate. Desire and distance, romantic illusion and physical reality.  Desire and distance as form of connection for the lonely. Loneliness seeking what contact it can find. Romantic illusion and physical reality both valid, both true. Mutual observation, each giving the other something. Tenderness in unlikely places, gentleness in what could be sordid. The limp revealed but not mocked. Fireworks and deflation. Two solitudes briefly touching.
meaning.inverted: Desire as exploitation of the vulnerable. Loneliness as permanent condition, unable to make real contact. Illusion as delusion preventing authentic connection. Voyeurism without mutuality. Tenderness as sentimentality masking use. The limp as defining flaw, reducing person to disability. Pleasure followed by shame. Solitudes confirmed, made worse by failed attempt.

visuals.detailed_description: The beach at twilight, divided compositionally. On one side, Gerty MacDowell sits on rocks in a romantically-lit scene - soft focus, pastel colors (especially purple), her pose displayful, her face pretty and dreaming. She watches a church service, Virgin Mary visible in the church. Romance novel aesthetics - everything idealized, sentimental. On the other side, Bloom sits at a distance, watching, his face showing desire but also loneliness and tenderness. The realistic side - harder focus, actual beach textures. Above, fireworks explode from the Mirus bazaar, their light illuminating both. As Gerty walks away in the foreground, her limp is visible - she's lame - but rendered without cruelty. The moment is both: romantic illusion (her view) and physical reality (his view), and both are treated with tenderness. Two lonely people giving each other a moment. The sea between them.

meaning.upright: 10pm at the Maternity Hospital. Bloom visits Mina Purefoy in labor. Medical students including Stephen drink and blaspheme. The episode chronicles English prose evolution from Anglo-Saxon through contemporary slang, paralleling nine-month gestation. Birth, creation, language evolution. Bloom the compassionate adult among drunken students.  Gestation and birth as sacred despite irreverence. Evolution of language as evolution of consciousness. Compassion amidst irreverence as moral center. The sacred and profane intertwined, inseparable. Creation's long labor honored. Styles succeeding styles, each valid in its time. Life emerging from pain, worth the wait. The adult in room of children, patient and present.
meaning.inverted: Gestation as endless without result. Evolution as degradation, each style worse. Compassion as weakness among the cruel. Sacred debased with nothing to replace it. Labor without birth, effort without result. Style as mere pastiche, empty imitation. Life stillborn, suffering pointless. Adult as mere outsider, unable to connect or influence.

visuals.detailed_description: The card is divided into horizontal bands or evolving sections, each rendered in the visual style of its corresponding prose era. Top/beginning: Anglo-Saxon simplicity - bold outlines, limited colors, medieval manuscript aesthetics. A woman (Mina Purefoy) in labor, suffering and sacred. Middle sections evolve: Gothic illumination, Renaissance perspective, Enlightenment clarity, Victorian sentimentality, each showing the ongoing birth. Throughout, in an adjoining room, medical students drink and blaspheme - rendered in the style of their section. Bloom moves through the styles, always recognizable, compassionate, the adult presence. By the bottom/end: contemporary 1904 realism or even modernist fragmentation, modern slang as text. The birth completes. The Oxen of the Sun (sacred creation) are shown slaughtered in background imagery - profanation of the sacred. Language itself is visible as text fragments evolving across the card. The overall effect: time moving forward, style evolving, life being born through pain and irreverence.

meaning.upright: Midnight in Nighttown. Bloom and Stephen meet in Dublin's red-light district. Hallucinatory phantasmagoria - unconscious desires, guilts, fears made manifest. Stephen's dead mother rises. Bloom transforms. Stephen smashes a chandelier, is knocked down by a soldier. Bloom tends him. The unconscious unleashed, transformation and transmutation, nighttown's revelations.  The unconscious unleashed as necessary revelation. Guilt and desire made visible to be acknowledged. Transformation and transmutation as possibilities. The dead accusing the living must be faced. Breaking through to truth even when violent. Meeting in the underworld changes both. Tending the fallen as love's expression. Nighttown reveals what daylight hides.
meaning.inverted: The unconscious as tyrant, making life impossible. Guilt and desire as prison with no escape. Transformation as degradation only. The dead as unappeasable, accusation eternal. Breaking as only destruction, nothing new created. Meeting without recognition or change. Enabling rather than helping, preventing growth. Revelations as trauma that cannot be integrated.

visuals.detailed_description: Nighttown explodes across the card in expressionistic chaos. Red light district - brothels, gas lamps casting red light, shadows distorted and threatening. Reality dissolves: Bloom appears multiple times in transformation - as woman in elaborate dress, as lord mayor in regalia, as defendant on trial, faces shifting and morphing. Stephen's dead mother rises from the ground, her face both loving and accusatory, decaying, holding out her hands. Stephen recoils, drunk, anguished. He smashes a chandelier with his ashplant, shards exploding, shouting 'Nothung!' A British soldier punches him down. At the bottom, Bloom kneels beside fallen Stephen, tender, protective - the real action among hallucinations. Throughout: pigs, whores, authority figures, all the unconscious desires and fears made visible. Theatrical stage directions visible as text. The overall effect: overwhelming, hallucinatory, nightmarish but also revelatory. German Expressionist angles and shadows, everything unstable.

meaning.upright: 1am at the cabman's shelter. Bloom and Stephen, exhausted after Circe, drink coffee and eat bread. Bloom tries to connect, suggests collaboration. A sailor tells improbable stories. Exhaustion permeates everything including prose. They discuss music, nationalism, identity. Stephen not quite hearing Bloom's wisdom. Failed communion, attempted connection.  Exhaustion after ordeal as honest condition. Attempted connection despite exhaustion's difficulty. Coffee and conversation as what's possible. The wisdom not quite received but offered nonetheless. Stories possibly true, possibly false, but companionship real. Shelter from night literal and metaphorical. The father figure offering help without force. The gap remaining but narrowed.
meaning.inverted: Exhaustion as defeat, unable to recover. Connection impossible across unbridgeable gaps. Empty conversation, words meaning nothing. Wisdom falling on deaf ears, wasted. Stories certainly false, companionship illusory. Shelter as trap, keeping you from home. Father figure as burden not help. Gap as chasm that grows wider.

visuals.detailed_description: The shabby interior of a cabman's shelter, all browns and greys, washed out by exhaustion and bad lighting. Bloom and Stephen sit at a stained table with thick coffee cups, bread crusts. Both look exhausted - Stephen drunk and hollow-eyed, Bloom weary but still kind. A sailor, probably named Murphy, sits nearby telling improbable stories about tattooed women and foreign ports - his face both trustworthy and untrustworthy. Other cabmen sleep or sit in background. The image itself should show signs of exhaustion - perhaps elements repeated accidentally, visual clichés (the moon seen through window is a perfect circle, too perfect, stock imagery), blurred edges suggesting tired vision. A newspaper with errors visible. Outside the window, dawn approaches but hasn't arrived - it's that dead hour of early morning. The attempted connection between Bloom and Stephen is visible in their postures - Bloom leaning in, offering, Stephen present but not quite receiving. Failed communion but not cruel failure.

meaning.upright: 2am, Bloom and Stephen walk to 7 Eccles Street. Bloom makes cocoa. They urinate together under stars. Discuss various topics in catechistic Q&A. Stephen declines to stay. They part. Bloom enters, finds evidence of Boylan, goes to bed beside Molly. Homecoming, catechism of the everyday, questions and answers.  Homecoming as arriving at what was always there. Catechism of the everyday revealing sacred in mundane. Urinating under stars as perfect communion. Offered hospitality declined without offense. Evidence noted and accepted with equanimity. Two men parting as friends almost were. The marriage bed as destination and acceptance. Questions and answers as form of love.
meaning.inverted: Homecoming as defeat, returning to what wounded you. Catechism as evasion, forms without content. Urination as degradation not communion. Hospitality rejected as judgment. Evidence as wound that cannot heal. Parting as failure, connection missed. The marriage bed as crime scene. Questions without answers, answers without meaning.

visuals.detailed_description: The card is organized as cold, precise, almost diagrammatic. Two men (Bloom and Stephen) walk through Dublin streets at night, rendered in geometric precision. They arrive at 7 Eccles Street. Interior: Bloom making cocoa, the process shown step-by-step almost instructionally. In the garden, both men stand urinating together under stars - the 'heaventree of stars hung with humid nightblue fruit' rendered as actual astronomical chart or star map, beautiful and distant. A large question mark is prominent - the catechistic structure made visible. They part at the doorway, one entering, one leaving. Bedroom: Molly's warm form in bed (asleep, substantial). Bloom noting evidence of Boylan - perhaps an indent in the bed, a lingering scent suggested visually. But no judgment, just observation. Mathematical equations float in the imagery - distances, measurements, the attempt to understand emotionally complex things through scientific precision. The overall effect: restrained, precise, but the emotion breaking through the cold form. Homecoming achieved.

meaning.upright: Molly Bloom's interior monologue, unpunctuated, flowing. She lies beside Leopold, thinking about Boylan, past lovers, her father, daughter Milly, upcoming tour, Leopold's oddness and kindness, his proposal on Howth Head. Eight enormous sentences. Ends 'yes I said yes I will Yes.' The feminine principle, affirmation despite everything.  The feminine principle as earth and affirmation. Affirmation despite everything that might deny it. Unpunctuated flow as consciousness itself. Sexual honesty without shame. Memory and present merged seamlessly. The body's wisdom speaking. Howth Head proposal remembered with love. Yes as ultimate word, ultimate meaning.
meaning.inverted: Flow as chaos without form or direction. Affirmation as delusion, denying reality. Unpunctuated as formless, unable to cohere. Sexuality as only truth, nothing else real. Memory as prison, past devouring present. Body without spirit, flesh without meaning. Proposal as trap remembered with bitterness. Yes as resignation not affirmation.

visuals.detailed_description: Molly lies in bed, substantial and real, her body taking up significant space in the composition. But the boundaries of the image blur and flow - nothing is hard-edged. Her consciousness streams across the card unpunctuated: images of Boylan's body, Leopold's proposal on Howth Head, her father in Gibraltar, her daughter Milly, past lovers, her upcoming concert tour, all bleeding into each other. The body is prominent and honestly rendered - flesh, warmth, the reality of embodiment. Text might flow through the image, all lowercase, no periods: 'yes I said yes I will Yes' appearing multiple times, growing. Howth Head with rhododendrons, the moment of proposal, young Molly saying yes. Present and past merge completely. The overall palette: warm flesh tones, pink, peach, the colors of the body and bed and flowers. Everything flows together - consciousness as river. The final, largest word: YES. Affirmation as ultimate principle.

meaning.upright: The final words of Ulysses: geographic signature of composition. Joyce writing in exile, 1914-1921, across three cities during World War I. The novel as artifact of specific times and places. The author's signature acknowledging the work's embodied creation. The geographic marker as bookend.  The work completed after years of labor. Exile as creative space making art possible. Geographic signature acknowledging embodied creation. Years of labor honored by marking. The author stepping forward to be counted. Three cities, one work, unified. History and art intersecting. The ending that frames the whole giving perspective.
meaning.inverted: Completion as abandonment, glad to be done. Exile as wound that cannot heal. Geography as limitation, each city a failure to stay. Labor as burden resented. The author imposing himself, breaking the spell. Fragmentation unresolved, three cities separate not unified. History as interruption of art. Ending that fails to close, leaving loose.

visuals.detailed_description: A map of Europe, sepia-toned like an archival document, showing three cities highlighted: Trieste, Zurich, Paris. In three vignettes or overlaid: Joyce writing in each city - in Trieste before the war, in Zurich during it (neutral Switzerland), in Paris after. He ages across the scenes, years passing, 1914-1921. War is suggested in the background - trenches, explosions, the Great War as context. Manuscripts pile up, pages accumulate. Finally, prominently at the bottom of the card, as if signed: 'Trieste-Zurich-Paris / 1914-1921' in Joyce's hand. A pen, a signature, three city names. The work is done. The geographic signature as acknowledgment: this is where and when it was made, in exile, through war, across years and cities. Simple, declarative, after all the complexity before it.

meaning.upright: Opening word of Finnegans Wake: 'riverrun, past Eve and Adam's, from swerve of shore to bend of bay, brings us by a commodius vicus of recirculation back to Howth Castle and Environs.' The Liffey, the cycle, eternal return. The last sentence loops back to first. Language becomes river, dream, night. After Ulysses comes this.  Eternal return, the cycle completing and beginning. The river of language flowing forever. After day comes dream, after waking sleep. Cycle completing invites restart. Everything connected through the river. The wake as both vigil and arising. Past Eve and Adam's, before and after. Beginning again, always beginning.
meaning.inverted: Return as repetition, condemned to relive. River as drowning, language dissolving into chaos. Dream as nightmare, sleep as death. Cycle as trap with no escape. Connection as confusion, everything merged meaninglessly. Wake as death only, no arising. Before Eden and unable to enter. Beginning that cannot progress, stuck at start.

visuals.detailed_description: The Liffey flows across the entire card, dominant, the river as protagonist. But the water is made of words, text, letters dissolving and reforming - portmanteau words visible as merged images (bababadalgharaghtakamminarronnkonnbronntonnerronntuonnthunntrovarrhounawnskawntoohoohoordenenthurnuk spelled out in waves). The river has no clear beginning or end - it loops, suggesting the circular structure where the last sentence becomes the first. Along the river: Eve and Adam's church, Howth Castle in the distance, Dublin landmarks dissolving into dream. It's night - after Ulysses's day comes the Wake's night. The water reflects stars but also contains them, sky and river merged. Languages merge visually - English, Irish, Latin, German, words combining into impossible hybrids. The overall effect: the river flowing eternally, language as river, consciousness as flow, the cycle that never ends. 'riverrun' prominent at both top and bottom of the card, or forming a circle. After day, dream. After Ulysses, this.

meaning.upright: The artist returns to the tower - but has it ever truly been home? Stephen's failed homecoming to the Martello Tower. The ambition to transcend meets the place you started. Arrival at the tower reveals it was always a fortress against connection, not a home to return to. The heroic struggle confronts the question: what if the place of origin cannot receive what you've become?  Returning to your place of intellectual or artistic origin to find it no longer fits. The recognition that home was always a fortress built by ambition, not a place of genuine belonging. Arrival reveals what departure was: necessary. The tower as beautiful prison. Understanding that transcendence meant leaving, not ascending.
meaning.inverted: Returning to the tower and convincing yourself it's still home despite all evidence. Ambition's fortress becoming actual prison. The place of origin as identity's entirety - unable to imagine beyond it. Homecoming as trap you walk into knowingly. Arrival as defeat disguised as return.

visuals.detailed_description: The Martello Tower rises against a grey Irish sky, stone and imposing. A figure (Stephen-like, thin, in black) stands at its threshold with an old-fashioned key in hand - but the posture is ambiguous: arriving or leaving? The tower's crenellations are sharp against clouds. The door is both opening and closing. Inside, barely visible, the cramped round room of intellectual ambition. Outside, the sea and wider world. The key itself is prominent in the foreground, large enough to see detail - it represents both access and what must be surrendered. The overall feeling: this was never truly home, just a tower one climbed. The alpha-omega point reveals itself as departure disguised as return.

meaning.upright: One tower gives way to another. The young artist's tower abandoned for... what? Another form of vertical ambition? The substitution of one intellectual fortress for a different one. Stephen for Bloom means even towers must be replaced - resistance in new forms, new heights to climb.  Recognizing that one form of intellectual ambition must give way to another. The tower you climbed no longer serves - a new tower, different but still vertical, calls. Substitution of resistance styles. Understanding that transcendence itself comes in stages, each requiring its own fortress-to-abandon. The old tower making way for new forms of reaching upward.
meaning.inverted: Replacing one prison with another, calling it growth. New tower same as old tower with different stones. Substitution without transformation - same ambition, same isolation, different elevation. The endless replacement of fortresses, never questioning why you need them. Mistaking change of tower for change of self.

visuals.detailed_description: Two towers stand in the composition. The left tower (perhaps Stephen's Martello) fades, becomes translucent, its stones losing solidity. The right tower rises or solidifies - different architecture but still vertical, still fortress-like. A thin figure in black stands between them, in motion, the moment of handoff visible. But both towers share the quality of isolation, of upward striving, of resistance through height. The sky behind them shows transition - dawn or dusk, one light giving way to another. The Tower suit symbol appears on both structures. The message: one form of ambition replaces another, but the vertical thrust continues.

meaning.upright: Three towers standing in relation - intellectual ambition requires trinity to stabilize. The tower of mind, the tower of art, the tower of refusal - Stephen's three-in-one. Resistance through triangulation. The sacred geometry of saying no to three different things simultaneously.  Understanding that ambition requires multiple sites of resistance. Three forms of intellectual striving that support each other: rejection of nation, rejection of church, rejection of art-as-received. The trinity that makes resistance stable rather than merely oppositional. Three towers creating a system of upward aspiration.
meaning.inverted: Three towers as three prisons, each reinforcing the others. Trinity as trap - nationality, language, religion entangling rather than challenging. The three forms of resistance becoming three forms of isolation. Ambition fractured across multiple impossibilities. The towers supporting each other in keeping you from the ground.

visuals.detailed_description: Three towers form a triangle in the composition. Each tower is distinct: one medieval (church), one modernist (art), one classical (nation) - but all share the vertical ambition, the crenellations, the fortress quality. Lines connect them like ley lines or paths, suggesting they form a system. At the center of the triangle, a figure stands (or struggles, or contemplates) - held in tension by three forms of resistance. The lighting differs on each tower: morning light on one, afternoon on another, night on the third - the three times of day, the three aspects of consciousness. The overall effect: resistance requires multiple sites, trinity stabilizes what replacement threatened.

meaning.upright: Dublin's spires, all its vertical ambitions. The city as collection of towers - churches, monuments, aspirations in stone. The bounded space contains multiple sites of resistance, multiple heights to climb or refuse. The four-square city reveals its towers.  Understanding the city as architecture of ambition. Every spire an aspiration, every tower a resistance. The bounded space contains multitudes of vertical striving. The map of intellectual possibility laid out in stone. Foundation that shows all the ways up and out.
meaning.inverted: The city as forest of prisons, every tower a trap. Multiple spires as multiple ways to be isolated. Architecture of ambition becoming architecture of confinement. The bounded space as labyrinth of failed transcendences. Every height climbed reveals another wall.

visuals.detailed_description: Dublin spreads below from an elevated viewpoint - the city as map and reality simultaneously. Every vertical element is emphasized: church spires (many), the Martello Tower at the edge, monuments, tall buildings, all the city's aspirations in stone. The Liffey bisects but doesn't dominate - this is about the vertical not horizontal. Streets form a grid (the four-square city) but the towers break the plane upward. Each tower casts a sharp shadow. A figure (tiny, Stephen-like) walks the streets below, dwarfed by all this vertical ambition. The overall effect: the city as container for resistance, every tower an option for climbing or refusing. The bounded space mapped in heights.

meaning.upright: The tower that doesn't fit among the city's spires. Stephen's tower stands alone, apart, marked as different. The intellectual outsider building vertical resistance while the city builds its own towers. The five-pointed ambition that won't tessellate with four-square aspiration.  Understanding your tower stands apart from the city's approved spires. Intellectual ambition as outsider status. Building upward in a way the city doesn't recognize or sanction. The disruption of being too vertical, too resistant, too apart. Seeing from the margin because your tower stands outside the grid.
meaning.inverted: The outsider tower as superiority - your spire higher because you're better. Isolation cultivated as proof of genius. The tower that doesn't fit because you won't fit, choosing alienation. Ambition that defines itself only through opposition to others' towers. Resistance as permanent identity rather than passing phase.

visuals.detailed_description: The Martello Tower stands at the edge of Dublin, separated from the city's cluster of spires by water or distance. Its round, fortress-like shape doesn't match the city's vertical Gothic spires - it's other, different, marked. A figure stands in its doorway or at its top, looking toward the city or turned away. The city's towers form a four-square pattern, but this tower sits at a fifth point - pentagram geometry, the star that won't fit the grid. The isolation is visible in the composition: space, distance, water between the outsider tower and the city's approved heights. But the tower stands nonetheless, vertical in its own way, resistance through difference.

meaning.upright: Forging in the smithy of the soul what tower you will build. Finding others who build towers like yours - recognizing each other across distance. The ambition that creates identity rather than discovering it. I refuse to serve, therefore I am this kind of tower-builder.  Forging your tower-identity through making it. Finding others who resist vertically as you do. Recognition that creates the category: we are tower-builders, artists, those who say non serviam. The smithy of the soul building the tower of the self. Identity as constructed ambition, made not found.
meaning.inverted: Identity as rigid tower, unable to change. Finding others to confirm your isolation, calling it recognition. The tower you build becoming who you are entirely - unable to imagine being ground-level. Forging as forcing - making yourself into the tower you think you should be. Identity as imprisonment in self-created height.

visuals.detailed_description: A smithy or forge in the foreground, fire bright in the cold angular world. On an anvil, plans for a tower - or the tower itself being forged in miniature, impossible but symbolic. A figure (Stephen-like) works the forge, creating their own tower-identity. In the middle distance, another tower stands - and the recognition is visible: these towers are alike, their builders recognize each other. Perhaps a line of sight or light connects them. The Tower suit symbol is being hammered into shape on the anvil. Behind everything, the formless materials that could become any shape - but are being made into towers, into vertical resistance. Identity as forged, not found.

meaning.upright: Seven towers when everyone else builds in multiples of two or four. The uneven number, the tower that makes the group unbalanced. Stephen's intellectual ambition as permanent asymmetry - always the odd tower out, always making the count come out wrong.  Understanding your tower makes the geometry unbalanced. Intellectual ambition that won't fit even numbers. Being the seventh tower when the city wants six. The asymmetry of resistance - you can't be split evenly, averaged out, balanced. Recognizing this is permanent, not temporary.
meaning.inverted: Asymmetry as identity entire - cultivating oddness as the point. The seventh tower built specifically to unbalance. Using uneven position as excuse for any behavior. Asymmetry fetishized - being different valued more than being anything specific. The odd tower out as only self-definition.

visuals.detailed_description: Seven towers arranged across the composition: three on one side (the Trinity of earlier cards), four on the other (the City's grid). Between them, a gap - the asymmetry visible and uncomfortable. No way to balance this, no even split possible. A figure associated with one of the seven towers stands in the gap or at the seventh position, creating the imbalance. Scales might be visible, unable to balance. The geometry itself resists harmony - angles that don't quite meet, proportions that feel wrong. The cold angular style of the suit now made deliberately uncomfortable. The message: this can't be made even, can't be balanced, won't resolve.

meaning.upright: All the towers ever built, pressing down. The nightmare of previous ambitions Stephen tries to wake from. But also: the accumulated height of all who climbed before, the ocean that might one day bring him home. Every tower built on ruins of earlier towers.  Understanding your tower stands on foundations of all previous towers. History of intellectual ambition as both burden and buoyancy. The nightmare of dead towers pressing down, but also the accumulation that makes your height possible. What brought you here: all who climbed before. Pattern revealing itself in tower-building across time.
meaning.inverted: History as crushing weight, all dead towers preventing your ascent. The past as nightmare only, no ocean to carry you. Towers built on ruins without acknowledging what you stand on. Pattern as predetermination - you must build the tower history demands. What chains you: all who climbed before and failed.

visuals.detailed_description: The composition shows towers through time, layered like geological strata or palimpsest. Ancient round towers, medieval spires, Martello towers, modern buildings - all vertical, all ambition, all shown simultaneously in transparent or overlapping layers. The deepest layers are ruins, foundations, the towers that failed. The newest layer is Stephen's tower, built on all that came before. A young figure struggles against or with waves that represent the ocean of history - the accumulated past as both nightmare dragging down and current that might eventually carry him home. Time is visible as compression: centuries of tower-building happening at once. The weight is palpable but also the foundation - you can only build this high because of all who built before.

meaning.upright: The moment just before the tower becomes something else. An invitation to see that height alone isn't the answer. The threshold between ambition and what ambition might serve. The breath before understanding that towers can be abandoned without falling.  Standing at your tower's highest point and seeing beyond it. The invitation to understand vertical isn't the only direction. Threshold moment - the tower has been climbed, what now? The breath before recognizing that resistance through height might give way to other forms. What's offered: the possibility that towers serve journeys, not replace them.
meaning.inverted: Invitation as trap - they want you to come down from your tower. Threshold as failure to commit to height. The moment before you refuse to see anything beyond vertical. The breath before doubling down on resistance. What's offered: comfort that would compromise your tower, so you refuse it.

visuals.detailed_description: At the top of a tower (Martello-style, crenellated), a door stands open. Not the door you enter - the door you might exit through. Beyond it, light and stars and suggestions of ground-level life - what happens beyond the tower's height. A figure stands at the threshold, neither in nor out, the moment of invitation held in suspension. Their posture shows recognition but not yet decision. The cold angular world of the tower meets something else: possibility, other directions than vertical. Stars overhead form a pattern, an offer. The breath before yes or no, before climbing down or refusing to, before the tower becomes what comes after towers.

meaning.upright: The realization: you will build this tower again. Viconian cycle of resistance - ambition returns, height calls again. Finishing one tower reveals you're at the base of the next. The eternal return of vertical striving. Riverrun past towers built and towers to come.  Understanding the cycle: towers will be built and abandoned and built again. The recurrence not as curse but as pattern recognized. Finishing one form of ambition reveals another calling. The tower climbed becoming the foundation for next tower. Acceptance that resistance returns, height calls eternally, the vertical thrust recurs. The yes to building again.
meaning.inverted: Condemned to build towers forever, unable to stop. The cycle as trap - must keep climbing, must keep resisting, never finding ground. Recurrence as curse. Each tower built just reveals you're imprisoned in tower-building. The wheel of ambition that crushes rather than carries. The eternal return of isolation.

visuals.detailed_description: Ten towers arrange themselves in a circle, each slightly different but all vertical, all resistant. The last tower connects to the first - its height becoming the foundation, the cycle explicit. Or alternatively: a spiral of towers ascending, but the highest point reveals itself as the lowest, the top as base. The Viconian cycle made architectural. A figure climbs or stands in this recursive space, understanding dawning: this is not escape, this is the pattern. Towers built, abandoned, built again. The river of time flows past towers that recur. The overall composition should be impossible - M.C. Escher-like - showing the return visually. The end is the beginning is the tower you're building now.

meaning.upright: Stephen as Child of Towers - young, unformed, brilliant but struggling. The artist before creation, all potential for tower-building untested. The son seeking the father but building upward instead. Youth as pure resistance, ambition before wisdom.  Youthful intellectual ambition before testing. The Child of Towers builds high but hasn't yet learned what towers are for. Unformed brilliance seeking height as answer. Resistance as growth stage - necessary but not final. The seeker who builds up because they don't know how else to seek. Pure potential for vertical striving.
meaning.inverted: Youth arrested in tower-building, never growing beyond resistance. Potential wasted in climbing that goes nowhere. The eternal Child, building towers but never becoming architect. Ambition as defense against maturity. Resistance as only mode because growth is too frightening. The seeker who builds walls.

visuals.detailed_description: A young man, thin and sharp in black (unmistakably Stephen), stands before or within a tower that's incomplete - scaffolding visible, stones partially laid, the structure ambitious but unfinished. Books are everywhere: stacked as building materials, used as foundation, opened as blueprints. The tower is his intellectual ambition made physical but not yet realized. His face shows brilliance but also struggle, certainty and doubt simultaneously. The Martello Tower visible in background, and this unfinished tower in foreground - the aspiration and the actuality. Tools of tower-building: pen as chisel, books as bricks. The Child of Towers has the ambition but not yet the wisdom to know what towers are for beyond reaching upward.

meaning.upright: The Father of Towers has built and abandoned towers, learned what they're for and what they're not. Mature ambition that knows height alone doesn't save you. The father who could teach the child but the child won't listen. Wisdom about resistance earned through years of resisting.  Mature understanding of intellectual ambition and its limits. The Father of Towers has climbed high enough to see that height alone isn't wisdom. Patient with those still building, knowing they must learn themselves. Wisdom earned through resistance and its failures. The offer to teach what towers are for, made without force. Paternal care for the young tower-builders.
meaning.inverted: Father-wisdom as bitterness - 'I climbed and failed, so will you.' Maturity as having given up on towers entirely, unable to remember why height mattered. Patience become passivity, unable to offer what might help. Teaching the wrong lessons about ambition. Father as failed example not guide.

visuals.detailed_description: A middle-aged man stands in the foreground, kind-eyed, weary but patient. Behind him: towers he has built and abandoned, visible at various stages - some complete, some partial, some ruined. He understands towers now in a way the Child cannot yet. His posture is not defeated but realistic - he knows what height can and cannot do. Perhaps he holds building tools, offering them, or perhaps he's set them down. The angular sharp world of towers is still present but softened by experience and time. He stands at ground level, having descended from heights, able to offer wisdom about climbing and descending both. The Father of Towers who could teach if the child would listen.
